one_line_takeaway: 1-SHUI is a new machine-unlearning benchmark requiring identity forgetting from a single support image with zero access to training data, and the accompanying meta-learned method MetaUnlearn is the most consistent (though not universally best) of the tested approaches across CelebA-HQ, CelebA, and MUFAC.
key_finding: Across the six main configurations (CelebA-HQ/20, CelebA-HQ/50, CelebA/5, CelebA/10, MUFAC/5, MUFAC/10), existing data-absence-compatible unlearning methods (SSD, PGU, JiT) are inconsistent: SSD collapses retain/test accuracy on CelebA and MUFAC (e.g., CelebA/5 test mAP drops to 23.1 vs. pretrain 80.9, ToW of 8.4), JiT fails to unlearn on CelebA-HQ/50 (test mAP drops 16.4 points, ToW 59.2) and shows near-zero forgetting on MUFAC (ToW 70.1-70.3, close to pretrain's 70.3). MetaUnlearn is the only method that improves ToW over the pretrain model in 5 of 6 settings and is consistently among the best or second-best across all six, e.g., CelebA-HQ/50 ToW 95.9 vs. pretrain's 94.8 vs. SSD's 73.7; MUFAC/10 ToW 79.1 (only method besides PGU to beat pretrain's 76.7). In the extreme NS=1 (single-identity) setting, MetaUnlearn achieves the best ToW on CelebA-HQ (97.4) and the lowest MIA rate on both CelebA-HQ and CelebA (0.01%). A follow-up analysis (Section 5.4) finds unlearning difficulty correlates with the Euclidean distance between the Support Sample and the forget identity's feature centroid: the mAP gap with a fully retrained model grows from -1.1 to 2.4 as this distance increases, and a Streisand Effect (over-forgetting relative to the retrained model, itself a membership leak) can occur when the Support Sample is very close to the identity centroid.
core_contribution: The paper introduces One-Shot Unlearning of Personal Identities (1-SHUI), a new machine-unlearning benchmark task in which the unlearning algorithm has NO access to the original training data (retain or forget sets) at unlearning time and must instead unlearn an identity given only a single 'Support Sample' image of that person. It also proposes MetaUnlearn, a meta-learning method that, while training data is still available, trains an MLP-parameterized 'meta-loss' h_phi(.) by repeatedly simulating unlearning requests (sampling identities, building a Support Set, taking one gradient step using the meta-loss, and evaluating an auxiliary alignment loss against forget/validation losses and mAP-weighted accuracy). Once trained, MetaUnlearn can unlearn any identity at deployment time using only that identity's single Support Sample and one gradient update, after the original dataset has been discarded.
threat_model: Not a security/attack threat model. The paper's motivating scenario is a regulatory/privacy-compliance one: data controllers must delete personal (identity) data from trained models under 'right to be forgotten' rules (citing EU Directive 95/46/EC Article 6) but may have already discarded the underlying training data by the time a deletion request arrives, leaving only a single portrait photo supplied by the requester. The paper does treat membership inference attacks (MIA) as an evaluation tool (measuring true-positive rate at 0.01% false-positive rate) to check whether unlearned identities remain distinguishable from unseen data, and discusses the 'Streisand Effect' (over-aggressive unlearning that itself leaks membership), but MIA here is used purely as a privacy-audit metric for the unlearning method, not as a novel attack technique the paper is proposing or defending against.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 6): (1) the work focuses only on a single unlearning request at a time — evaluating multiple simultaneous/sequential unlearning requests under data absence is left to future work; (2) the study is restricted to face datasets (CelebA, CelebA-HQ, MUFAC) due to their availability and fine-grained identity/attribute annotations, and the authors note it would be useful to test on full-body images or images with multiple identities; (3) 1-SHUI as a task framework could in principle extend beyond personal identities to objects or animals, but the authors consider these less relevant from a regulatory standpoint and leave them unexplored. Empirically, MetaUnlearn also underperforms baselines in some CelebA settings (e.g., JiT scores a higher ToW than MetaUnlearn on CelebA/5, 97.6 vs. 97.2, in the NS=1 setting) and shows notable retain-accuracy degradation on MUFAC/10 (retain accuracy 96.3 vs. pretrain 99.9).

quantitative_results: CelebA-HQ/20: MetaUnlearn ToW 95.7 vs. PGU 95.6 vs. pretrain 95.5
CelebA-HQ/50: MetaUnlearn ToW 95.9 vs. pretrain 94.8 vs. SSD 73.7 vs. JiT 59.2
CelebA-HQ/20 MIA: SSD 0.03% vs. MetaUnlearn 0.04% vs. JiT 0.97% vs. PGU 0.17%
CelebA/5: JiT best ToW 97.6, MetaUnlearn 97.2, SSD test mAP collapses to 23.1 (ToW 8.4)
CelebA/10: MetaUnlearn best ToW 97.4 vs. PGU (second) 96.9
MUFAC/10: MetaUnlearn ToW 79.1 vs. PGU 79.0 vs. pretrain 76.7; MetaUnlearn forget accuracy drop of 7.9 points vs. pretrain
NS=1 single-identity CelebA-HQ: MetaUnlearn ToW 97.4 (best), MIA 0.01% (best)
Ablation (Table 5) on auxiliary loss: 'Second term + Accuracy' variant achieves ToW 95.7-97.3 across settings vs. SCRUB loss substitute which is unstable/OOM or scores as low as 51.7 (CelebA/5)
baselines_compared: SCRUB (Kurmanji et al., 2023) [reference, uses full dataset]
Bad Teacher (Chundawat et al., 2023a) [reference, uses full dataset]
